title: How much does it cost to heat a house in the UK?
description: What heating a UK home really costs in 2026: typical annual gas bills by house size, cost per day in winter, gas vs electric vs heat pump, and how to cut it.
blurb: Typical annual heating costs by house size, what a cold day costs, and how gas compares with electric and heat pumps.
lede: Heating is the single biggest energy cost in almost every UK home. It dwarfs lighting, cooking and gadgets put together, and it is the line on the bill that moves most when prices change or winter turns cold. Yet most people only ever see one merged number on a statement and have no feel for what the heating itself actually costs, per year, per day or per hour. This guide breaks it down with current price cap rates, so you can see where your home sits, what a cold snap really costs you, and which changes make a meaningful dent.
short_answer: A typical gas-heated home in Great Britain spends roughly £630 a year on space heating, within a total annual gas bill of about £949 once hot water, cooking and standing charges are included. That assumes Ofgem's typical medium usage of 11,500 kWh of gas at the July to September 2026 cap rate of 7.33p per kWh. A small, well-insulated flat might spend under £450 on heating, while a large or poorly insulated house can easily pass £1,200.

sections.h2: What a typical heating bill looks like in 2026
sections.html:
Ofgem publishes typical annual gas consumption figures for low, medium and high users, and they map loosely onto home size: a one or two bedroom flat, a two or three bedroom house, and a large four or five bedroom house. Around three quarters of the gas a typical gas-heated home burns goes on space heating, with the rest going to hot water and, in some homes, cooking. Here is what those usage bands cost at current cap rates.

Usage band (Ofgem typical values)Annual gas useGas unit costStanding chargeTotal annual gas billSpace heating share (approx 75%)
Low (small flat, 1-2 people)7,500 kWh£550£106£656about £410
Medium (2-3 bed house)11,500 kWh£843£106£949about £630
High (4-5 bed house)17,000 kWh£1,246£106£1,352about £935

At 7.33p per kWh for gas and a 29.04p daily standing charge, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026 (GB direct debit average). Checked June 2026.

Two caveats. First, these are averages across Great Britain, and your own unit rate and standing charge vary slightly by region and payment method. Second, the bands are about usage, not strictly bedrooms. A draughty two bed Victorian terrace can burn more gas than a well-insulated four bed new build, which is why the section on cost drivers below matters more than the label on the estate agent listing.

sections.h2: What actually drives your heating cost
sections.html:
Two identical-looking houses on the same street can have heating bills that differ by half. The gap comes from a handful of factors, roughly in order of impact:

  • Heat loss. Insulation levels in the loft and walls, the quality of the glazing and how draughty the building is determine how fast heat escapes. This is the biggest single factor and the reason an older solid-wall house costs so much more to keep warm than a 2010s build of the same size.
  • Floor area and layout. More volume means more air to heat and more external surface to lose it through. Detached houses lose heat from all four walls plus the roof; a mid-terrace or a flat gets free help from the neighbours.
  • Thermostat setting and hours of heating. Each degree on the thermostat adds somewhere near 10% to your heating cost over a season. The difference between 19C for six hours a day and 22C for twelve is enormous.
  • System efficiency. A modern condensing boiler running at a sensible flow temperature converts around 90% of the gas it burns into useful heat. An older boiler, or a new one set up badly, can sit closer to 80%.
  • Occupancy. Someone home all day heats for far more hours than a household that is out from eight until six.

If your bill feels high for your house type, work through these in order. Our wider heating guides cover each one, and whether to leave the heating on all day answers the most common scheduling question directly.

sections.h2: Gas, electric or heat pump: cost per unit of heat
sections.html:
The headline unit rates are misleading on their own, because heating systems differ in how much useful heat they squeeze from each kWh you pay for. Gas is cheap per kWh but a boiler wastes some of it up the flue. A plug-in electric heater turns every kWh into heat but pays the full electricity rate. A heat pump pays the electricity rate too, but moves three to four units of heat into your home for every unit it consumes. The fair comparison is the cost per kWh of heat delivered into your rooms.

SystemEfficiencyCost per kWh of heatCost for 10,000 kWh of heat per year
Gas boiler, typical (85%)85%8.6p£862
Gas boiler, well set up (90%)90%8.1p£814
Direct electric heaters100%26.1p£2,611
Air source heat pump, modest (SCOP 3.0)300%8.7p£870
Air source heat pump, good install (SCOP 3.5)350%7.5p£746
Air source heat pump, excellent install (SCOP 4.0)400%6.5p£653

At 26.11p per kWh electricity and 7.33p per kWh gas, Ofgem cap July to September 2026. Standing charges excluded. SCOP is the seasonal efficiency of a heat pump.

The pattern is clear. Heating a whole house with plug-in electric heaters costs roughly three times as much as gas, which is why they only make sense for short bursts in single rooms. A well-installed heat pump now undercuts a gas boiler on running cost at cap rates, and dedicated heat pump tariffs with cheaper off-peak electricity widen that gap further. If you are weighing one up, start with how heat pumps work before getting into quotes.

sections.h2: What heating costs per day and per hour in winter
sections.html:
Annual figures hide how lumpy heating is. A typical gas-heated home burns very little gas for heating between May and September, then a great deal between November and February. Smart meter data makes this vivid: the same house that uses 5 kWh of gas on a mild October day can use 80 kWh or more during a January cold snap.

As rough working figures for a medium house at current rates:

  • A mild autumn day with a couple of hours of heating: 15 to 25 kWh of gas, around £1.10 to £1.85.
  • A typical winter day with morning and evening heating: 40 to 60 kWh, around £2.95 to £4.40.
  • A cold snap day with long heating hours and sub-zero nights: 70 to 100 kWh, around £5.15 to £7.35.

Per hour, a gas boiler actively heating an average house burns somewhere between 5 and 12 kWh depending on its size, how cold it is and how well the house holds heat, which works out at roughly 35p to 90p an hour at 7.33p per kWh. We break that calculation down properly in what central heating costs per hour. The practical point: a cold week can cost more than a mild month, so judging a tariff or a new thermostat schedule on one week's data is a mistake in either direction.

sections.h2: How to bring the cost down
sections.html:
Because heating is such a large share of the bill, percentage savings here are worth far more than anywhere else in the house. In rough order of effort against payoff:

  • Turn the boiler flow temperature down. Most combi boilers leave the factory set far hotter than needed. Dropping the flow temperature to around 60C lets a condensing boiler actually condense, trimming gas use by several percent for free. See our guide to boiler flow temperature for the step by step.
  • Shave the thermostat. Each degree down saves somewhere near 10% of your heating cost. Try one degree for a week; most people stop noticing within days.
  • Tighten the schedule. Heat the hours you are home and awake, not the whole day. Modern boilers and well-insulated homes recover quickly.
  • Stop heating empty rooms. Thermostatic radiator valves let you run spare rooms several degrees cooler than living spaces.
  • Cut heat loss. Loft insulation topped up to 270mm, draught-proofing around doors and floors, and thick curtains closed at dusk all pay back quickly. Cavity wall insulation, where the house has unfilled cavities, is one of the best-value bigger jobs.
  • Maintain the system. Bleed radiators that are cold at the top and have the system balanced if some rooms heat much faster than others.

Stacked together, the free and cheap items on this list routinely take 10 to 20% off a heating bill, which on the medium house above is £90 to £190 a year before you spend anything on insulation.

At July to September 2026 cap rates, heating a typical gas-heated UK home costs around £630 a year, inside a total gas bill of about £949. Small flats sit nearer £410 for heating; large or leaky houses pass £935 and can go well beyond. Per unit of heat delivered, gas and a well-installed heat pump now cost broadly the same, while whole-house heating with plug-in electric heaters costs about three times as much. Your two biggest levers are how fast your house loses heat and how you run the system: flow temperature, thermostat setting and schedule. Get those right before spending money, then put any budget into insulation, where the savings repeat every winter for decades.

faq.q: How much does it cost to heat an average house in the UK per year?
faq.a: Around £630 a year for space heating in a typical medium gas-heated home, based on Ofgem's typical use of 11,500 kWh of gas a year, of which roughly three quarters goes on heating, at the July to September 2026 cap rate of 7.33p per kWh. The full gas bill including hot water and standing charges comes to about £949.

faq.q: How much does central heating cost per day in winter?
faq.a: A medium gas-heated house typically burns 40 to 60 kWh of gas on a normal winter day, which costs roughly £2.95 to £4.40 at 7.33p per kWh. During a cold snap that can rise to 70 to 100 kWh, or about £5.15 to £7.35 a day.

faq.q: Is electric heating more expensive than gas?
faq.a: For whole-house heating, yes, by roughly three times. Electricity costs 26.11p per kWh against 7.33p for gas at July to September 2026 cap rates, and even after allowing for boiler losses, gas heat costs around 8 to 9p per kWh delivered versus about 26p for plug-in electric heaters. A heat pump changes the picture because it delivers three to four units of heat per unit of electricity.

faq.q: Is a heat pump cheaper to run than a gas boiler?
faq.a: At current cap rates a well-installed air source heat pump achieving a seasonal efficiency of 3.5 or better costs about the same as or slightly less than a gas boiler per unit of heat, and it avoids the gas standing charge if you disconnect gas entirely. A poorly installed heat pump running below an efficiency of 3 can cost slightly more than gas.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to leave the heating on low all day?
faq.a: No. Your home loses heat in proportion to how warm it is, so keeping it warm for 24 hours means losing, and paying to replace, more heat than warming it only for the hours you need. Use a timer or smart thermostat so the heating runs when you are home and awake.
